URL: https://www.activesustainability.com/environment/what-are-bioplastics/

SUMMARY:
Bioplastics are biodegradable materials that come from renewable sources and can be used to reduce the problem of plastic waste that is suffocating the planet and contaminating the environment .

# What are bioplastics?

![alt-img-medio-ambiente](/media/567356/que-son-bioplasticos.jpg "title-img-medio-ambiente")

**Bioplastics are biodegradable materials that come from renewable sources and can be used to reduce the problem of plastic waste that is suffocating the planet and contaminating the environment.** Does it make any sense to continue using packaging that can take centuries to disappear to pack products that only last days or months?

**Plastic** is the third most commonly used **petroleum derivative** in the world; **each year 200 million tons of plastic are consumed on the planet.** It comes from a non-renewable source (petroleum), it is **contaminating and non-biodegradable** (it can take more than 1000 years to decompose).

![Sea turtles die from consuming plastic remains](/media/43955/sea-turtles-die-from-consuming-plastic-remains.jpg "Sea turtles die from consuming plastic remains")

![Sea turtles die from consuming plastic remains](/media/43955/sea-turtles-die-from-consuming-plastic-remains.jpg "Sea turtles die from consuming plastic remains")

That is why countries such as Bangladesh have prohibited traditional plastic bags (they jam the sewage pipes and cause flooding), Africa has baptized them as a new 'national flower' because they are so visible all over the landscape and Europe has considered taxing them. In addition, this waste is the **cause of the death of marine species and** fowl that ingest them (whales, sea turtles, albatross, etc.) and **pose a serious environmental problem, such as garbage patches** (islands of garbage).

## Bioplastic: An alternative to traditional plastic

![Birds dead from ingestion of plastic - Garbage Patch](/media/43956/birds-dead-from-ingestion-of-plastic-garbage-patch.jpg "Birds dead from ingestion of plastic - Garbage Patch")

![Birds dead from ingestion of plastic - Garbage Patch](/media/43956/birds-dead-from-ingestion-of-plastic-garbage-patch.jpg "Birds dead from ingestion of plastic - Garbage Patch")

As an alternative, the **use of bioplastics** is being promoted, consisting in **obtaining natural polymers from agricultural, cellulose or potato and corn starch waste.**

These are **100% degradable, equally resistant and versatile,** already used in **agriculture, textile industry, medicine and,** over all, in the **container and packaging market**, and biopolymers are already becoming popular in cities throughout Europe and the United States for ecological reasons: they are known as PHA.

This product **is expected to cover the needs of 10% of the European plastics market within 10 years**.

## PHA as bioplastic

These are **polyesters produced by fermenting raw vegetable materials with a series of bacterial strains**. For example, PHAs can be used for injection molding to build automobile parts and for many other uses. Specifically, PHA (polyhydroxyalkanoate) is extracted from bacteria such as pseudomonas. **In its natural form, it is similar to transparent kitchen film**, with the difference that it is an authentic bioplastic.

## Advantages of bioplastics:

## Uses of bioplastics:

![37% de la tierra cultivable para agricultura. La destinada a bioplásticos, es 0,02%.](/media/41446/european-bioplastics.jpg)

![37% de la tierra cultivable para agricultura. La destinada a bioplásticos, es 0,02%.](/media/41446/european-bioplastics.jpg)

It is expanding its use in various sectors: **medical** (prostheses, sutures ...) **in food** (catering products, disposable containers ...), **toys, and even in the world of fashion** (Versace has a clothing line Ingeo, made ​​from corn) and, of course, biodegradable bags.

![60-day biodegradable bags](/media/43958/60-day-biodegradable-bags.jpg "60-day biodegradable bags")

![60-day biodegradable bags](/media/43958/60-day-biodegradable-bags.jpg "60-day biodegradable bags")

*\* DID YOU KNOW ... Not all '**bioplastics**' are degradable: should only be called bioplastics those born from renewable biological sources, and they are biodegradable. Never petroleum. Neither should be considered as such those obtained by GMOs.*

### Información sobre protección de datos

En cumplimiento del Reglamento UE 2016/679 de Protección de Datos y demás
normativa vigente en materia de Protección de Datos, se le informa de que
sus datos de carácter personal serán tratados por Acciona, S.A. (en
adelante, ACCIONA), con los siguientes datos identificativos NIF: A08001851,
Dirección: Avenida de Europa, 18, Parque empresarial de la Moraleja, 28108
de Alcobendas (Madrid), Tel: +34 91 663 28 50, email: [protecciondedatos@acciona.com](mailto:protecciondedatos@acciona.com),
con la finalidad de atender sus comentarios y gestionar sus consultas,
solicitudes, reclamaciones o sugerencias, así como el envío, por medios
electrónicos, de información sobre nuestros servicios y productos, a través
del correo electrónico de contacto.

La base jurídica para el tratamiento de los datos es el consentimiento del
usuario al comunicarse con nosotros.

Los datos se conservarán mientras se mantenga la relación y no se solicite su
supresión y, en cualquier caso, nunca durante un plazo superior a doce
meses.

En caso de que su petición no sea dirigida a ACCIONA, sino a una entidad que
forma parte del Grupo Acciona, ésta comunicará los datos a la sociedad del
Grupo que pueda atender su solicitud de servicio o información de forma más
eficiente. En este sentido, la comunicación de estos datos puede constituir
una transferencia internacional, por estar estas empresas ubicadas en países
fuera del territorio de la Unión Europea, para poder atender las necesidades
de comunicación entre las personas que forman parte del Grupo a nivel
mundial. (Puede consultar un listado de empresas del Grupo en [www.acciona.com/es/accionistas-inversores/informacion-financiera/cuentas-anuales](https://www.acciona.com/es/accionistas-inversores/informacion-financiera/cuentas-anuales/)).
La aceptación de los términos de la privacidad supone el consentimiento para
la transferencia internacional de sus datos necesaria para la correcta
tramitación de su petición. No están previstas otras cesiones de datos,
salvo obligación legal.

El interesado puede ejercitar sus derechos de acceso, rectificación,
supresión, portabilidad y la limitación u oposición, ante Acciona, S.A.
dirigiéndose por escrito al Departamento de Protección de datos sita en
Avenida de Europa, 18, 28108 de Alcobendas (Madrid) o mediante el envío de
un correo electrónico en la siguiente dirección: [protecciondedatos@acciona.com](mailto:protecciondedatos@acciona.com),
adjuntando en ambos casos copia del DNI u otro documento identificativo.
Asimismo, podrá en cualquier momento, retirar el consentimiento prestado
dirigiéndose a la dirección arriba indicada, así como reclamar ante la
Autoridad de Control (Agencia Española de Protección de Datos [www.aepd.es](http://www.aepd.es/)).

Para más información ponemos a su disposición la [Política de Privacidad](https://acciona.com/es/politica-privacidad) de la Página.

--- SOURCE 2: European Bioplastics e.V. ---
URL: https://www.european-bioplastics.org/bioplastics/

SUMMARY:
According to European Bioplastics, a plastic material is defined as a bioplastic if it is either biobased, biodegradable, or features both

FULL CONTENT:
![European Bioplastics e.V. Logo](https://www.european-bioplastics.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/eubp-logo.png)
![European Bioplastics e.V. Logo](https://www.european-bioplastics.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/eubp-logo-mobile.png)
![](https://www.european-bioplastics.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/2.0_Granulat_in_Gla%CC%88sern_PLA_Header_slider.jpg)

# What are bioplastics?

**Bioplastics are a large family of different materials**  
Bioplastics are not just one single material. They comprise of a whole family of materials with different properties and applications. According to European Bioplastics, a plastic material is defined as a bioplastic if it is either biobased, biodegradable, or features both properties.

***Bioplastics are biobased, biodegradable, or both.***

**Biobased:** The term ‘biobased’ means that the material or product is (partly) derived from biomass (plants). Biomass used for bioplastics stems from e.g. corn, sugarcane, or cellulose.

![](https://www.european-bioplastics.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/EUBP_BiobasedRenewable_green-scaled.jpg)

![](https://www.european-bioplastics.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/EUBP_BiobasedRenewable_green-scaled.jpg)

**Biodegradable:** Biodegradation is a chemical process during which microorganisms that are available in the environment convert materials into natural substances such as water, carbon dioxide, and compost (artificial additives are not needed). The process of biodegradation depends on the surrounding environmental conditions (e.g. location or temperature), on the material and on the application.

***‘Biobased’ does not equal ‘biodegradable’***  
The property of biodegradation does not depend on the resource basis of a material but is rather linked to its chemical structure. In other words, 100 percent biobased plastics may be non-biodegradable, and 100 percent fossil based plastics can biodegrade.

**Benefits of bioplastics**  
Bioplastics are driving the evolution of plastics. There are two major advantages of biobased plastic products compared to their conventional versions: they save fossil resources by using biomass which regenerates (annually) and provides the unique potential of carbon neutrality. Furthermore, biodegradability is an add-on property of certain types of bioplastics. It offers additional means of recovery at the end of a product’s life.

Download our Fact Sheet on [What are bioplastics?](http://docs.european-bioplastics.org/publications/fs/EuBP_FS_What_are_bioplastics.pdf)

![](https://www.european-bioplastics.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/EUBP_MaterialCoordinateSystem-scaled.jpg)

--- SOURCE 3: Bioplastic - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bioplastic

SUMMARY:
Bioplastics are plastic materials produced from renewable biomass sources. Historically, bioplastics made from natural materials like shellac or cellulose

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# Bioplastic

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/BiodegradablePlasticUtensils1.jpg/250px-BiodegradablePlasticUtensils1.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Flower_Wrapping_made_of_PLA-Blend_Bio-Flex.jpg/250px-Flower_Wrapping_made_of_PLA-Blend_Bio-Flex.jpg)

**Bioplastics** are [plastic](/wiki/Plastic "Plastic") materials produced from renewable [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") sources. [Historically](/wiki/Timeline_of_plastic_development "Timeline of plastic development"), bioplastics made from natural materials like [shellac](/wiki/Shellac "Shellac") or [cellulose](/wiki/Celluloid "Celluloid") had been the first plastics. Since the end of the 19th century they have been increasingly superseded by [fossil-fuel](/wiki/Fossil-fuel "Fossil-fuel") plastics derived from [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") or [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") (*fossilized* biomass is not considered to be [renewable](/wiki/Renewable_resource "Renewable resource") in reasonable short time). Today, in the context of [bioeconomy](/wiki/Bioeconomy "Bioeconomy") and [circular economy](/wiki/Circular_economy "Circular economy"), bioplastics are gaining interest again. Conventional petro-based polymers are increasingly blended with bioplastics to manufacture "bio-attributed" or "mass-balanced" plastic products - so the difference between bio- and other plastics might be difficult to define.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Bioplastics can be produced by:

One advantage of bioplastics is their independence from [fossil fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") as a raw material, which is a finite and globally unevenly distributed resource linked to [petroleum politics](/wiki/Petroleum_politics "Petroleum politics") and [environmental impacts](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_the_petroleum_industry "Environmental impact of the petroleum industry"). Bioplastics can utilize previously unused waste materials (e.g., [straw](/wiki/Straw "Straw"), [woodchips](/wiki/Woodchips "Woodchips"), [sawdust](/wiki/Sawdust "Sawdust"), and [food waste](/wiki/Food_waste "Food waste")). [Life cycle analysis](/wiki/Life_cycle_analysis "Life cycle analysis") studies show that some bioplastics can be made with a lower [carbon footprint](/wiki/Carbon_footprint "Carbon footprint") than their fossil counterparts, for example when [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") is used as raw material and also for energy production. However, other bioplastics' processes are less efficient and result in a higher carbon footprint than fossil plastics.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Whether any kind of plastic is degradable or non-degradable (durable) depends on its molecular structure, not on whether or not the biomass constituting the raw material is fossilized. Both durable bioplastics, such as [Bio-PET](/wiki/Bio-PET "Bio-PET") or [biopolyethylene](/wiki/Biopolyethylene "Biopolyethylene") (bio-based analogues of fossil-based [polyethylene terephthalate](/wiki/Polyethylene_terephthalate "Polyethylene terephthalate") and [polyethylene](/wiki/Polyethylene "Polyethylene")), and degradable bioplastics, such as [polylactic acid](/wiki/Polylactic_acid "Polylactic acid"), [polybutylene succinate](/wiki/Polybutylene_succinate "Polybutylene succinate"), or [polyhydroxyalkanoates](/wiki/Polyhydroxyalkanoates "Polyhydroxyalkanoates"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) exist.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Bioplastics must be recycled similar to fossil-based plastics to avoid [plastic pollution](/wiki/Plastic_pollution "Plastic pollution"); "drop-in" bioplastics (such as biopolyethylene) fit into existing recycling streams. On the other hand, recycling biodegradable bioplastics in the current recycling streams poses additional challenges, as it may raise the cost of sorting and decrease the yield and the quality of the recyclate. However, biodegradation is not the only acceptable end-of-life disposal pathway for biodegradable bioplastics, and mechanical and chemical recycling are often the preferred choice from the environmental point of view.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[Biodegradability](/wiki/Biodegradable "Biodegradable") may offer an end-of-life pathway in certain applications, such as agricultural mulch, but the concept of biodegradation is not as straightforward as many believe. Susceptibility to biodegradation is highly dependent on the chemical backbone structure of the polymer, and different bioplastics have different structures, thus it cannot be assumed that bioplastic in the environment will readily disintegrate. Conversely, biodegradable plastics can also be synthesized from fossil fuels.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

As of 2018, bioplastics represented approximately 2% of the global plastics output (>380 million tons).[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2022, the commercially most important types of bioplastics were [PLA](/wiki/Polylactic_acid "Polylactic acid") and products based on [starch](/wiki/Starch "Starch").[[14]](#cite_note-14) With continued research on bioplastics, investment in bioplastic companies and rising scrutiny on fossil-based plastics, bioplastics are becoming more dominant in some markets, while the output of fossil plastics also steadily increases.

## IUPAC definition

The [International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry](/wiki/International_Union_of_Pure_and_Applied_Chemistry "International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry") define biobased polymer as:

*Biobased polymer* derived from the *[biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass")* or issued from monomers derived from the biomass and which, at some stage in its processing into finished products, can be shaped by flow.

## Proposed applications

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Bio-K_Abfallbeutel_Kompostbeutel_CG.jpg/250px-Bio-K_Abfallbeutel_Kompostbeutel_CG.jpg)

Few commercial applications exist for bioplastics. Cost and performance remain problematic. Typical is the example of Italy, where biodegradable plastic bags are compulsory for shoppers since 2011 with the introduction of a specific law.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Beyond structural materials, electroactive bioplastics are being developed that promise to [carry electric current](/wiki/Organic_electronics "Organic electronics").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Bioplastics are used for disposable items, such as [packaging](/wiki/Packaging "Packaging"), crockery, cutlery, pots, bowls, and straws.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[Biopolymers](/wiki/Biopolymer "Biopolymer") are available as coatings for paper rather than the more common petrochemical coatings.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Bioplastics called **drop-in bioplastics** are chemically identical to their fossil-fuel counterparts but made from renewable resources. Examples include [bio-PE](/wiki/Bio-PE "Bio-PE"), [bio-PET](/wiki/Bio-PET "Bio-PET"), [bio-propylene](/wiki/Propylene "Propylene"), [bio-PP](/wiki/Bio-PP "Bio-PP"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) and biobased nylons.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Drop_in_bioplastics-23) Drop-in bioplastics are easy to implement technically, as existing infrastructure can be used.[[24]](#cite_note-24) A dedicated bio-based pathway allows to produce products that cannot be obtained through traditional chemical reactions and can create products which have unique and superior properties, compared to fossil-based alternatives.[[23]](#cite_note-Drop_in_bioplastics-23)

## Types

### Polysaccharide-based bioplastics

#### Starch-based plastics

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/St%C3%A4rke-Packstoff_P%C3%B6mpel_CG.jpg/250px-St%C3%A4rke-Packstoff_P%C3%B6mpel_CG.jpg)

[Thermoplastic](/wiki/Thermoplastic "Thermoplastic") starch represents the most widely used bioplastic, constituting about 50 percent of the bioplastics market.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Simple starch bioplastic film can be made at home by [gelatinizing starch](/wiki/Starch_gelatinization "Starch gelatinization") and [solution casting](/wiki/Polymer_solution_casting "Polymer solution casting").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Pure starch is able to absorb [humidity](/wiki/Humidity "Humidity"), and is thus a suitable material for the production of drug capsules by the pharmaceutical sector. However, pure starch-based bioplastic is brittle. [Plasticizer](/wiki/Plasticiser "Plasticiser") such as [glycerol](/wiki/Glycerol "Glycerol"), glycol, and [sorbitol](/wiki/Sorbitol "Sorbitol") can also be added so that the starch can also be processed thermo-plastically.[[27]](#cite_note-:1-27) The characteristics of the resulting bioplastic (also called "thermoplastic starch") can be tailored to specific needs by adjusting the amounts of these additives. Conventional polymer processing techniques can be used to process starch into bioplastic, such as extrusion, injection molding, compression molding and solution casting.[[27]](#cite_note-:1-27) The properties of starch bioplastic is largely influenced by [amylose](/wiki/Amylose "Amylose")/[amylopectin](/wiki/Amylopectin "Amylopectin") ratio. Generally, high-amylose starch results in superior mechanical properties.[[28]](#cite_note-28) However, high-amylose starch has less processability because of its higher gelatinization temperature[[29]](#cite_note-29) and higher melt viscosity.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Starch-based bioplastics are often blended with biodegradable polyesters to produce starch/polylactic acid,[[31]](#cite_note-31) starch/[polycaprolactone](/wiki/Polycaprolactone "Polycaprolactone")[[32]](#cite_note-32) or starch/Ecoflex[[33]](#cite_note-33) (polybutylene adipate-co-terephthalate produced by [BASF](/wiki/BASF "BASF")[[34]](#cite_note-34)) blends. These blends are used for industrial applications and are also compostable. Other producers, such as [Roquette](/wiki/Roquette_Fr%C3%A8res "Roquette Frères"), have developed other starch/[polyolefin](/wiki/Polyolefin "Polyolefin") blends. These blends are not biodegradable, but have a lower carbon footprint than petroleum-based plastics used for the same applications.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Starch is cheap, abundant, and renewable.[[36]](#cite_note-auto-36)

Starch-based films (mostly used for packaging purposes) are made mainly from starch blended with thermoplastic polyesters to form biodegradable and compostable products. These films are seen specifically in consumer goods packaging of magazine wrappings and bubble films. In [food packaging](/wiki/Food_packaging "Food packaging"), these films are seen as bakery or fruit and vegetable bags. Composting bags with this films are used in selective collecting of organic waste.[[36]](#cite_note-auto-36) Further, starch-based films can be used as a paper.[[37]](#cite_note-StarchPlasticsPaperUsda-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Starch-based nanocomposites have been widely studied, showing improved mechanical properties, thermal stability, moisture resistance, and gas barrier properties.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

#### Cellulose-based plastics

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Bio-K_Blister_CG.jpg/250px-Bio-K_Blister_CG.jpg)

[Cellulose](/wiki/Cellulose "Cellulose") bioplastics are mainly the [cellulose esters](/wiki/Cellulose_ester "Cellulose ester") (including [cellulose acetate](/wiki/Cellulose_acetate "Cellulose acetate") and [nitrocellulose](/wiki/Nitrocellulose "Nitrocellulose")) and their derivatives, including [celluloid](/wiki/Celluloid "Celluloid").

Cellulose can become thermoplastic when extensively modified. An example of this is cellulose acetate, which is expensive and therefore rarely used for packaging. However, cellulosic fibers added to starches can improve mechanical properties, permeability to gas, and water resistance due to being less hydrophilic than starch.[[36]](#cite_note-auto-36)

### Protein-based plastics

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Edible_packaging_film.jpg/250px-Edible_packaging_film.jpg)

Bioplastics can be made from proteins from different sources. For example, wheat gluten and casein show promising properties as a raw material for different biodegradable polymers.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Additionally, soy protein is being considered as another source of bioplastic. Soy proteins have been used in plastic production for over one hundred years. For example, body panels of an [original Ford automobile](/wiki/Soybean_car "Soybean car") were made of soy-based plastic.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

There are difficulties with using soy protein-based plastics due to their water sensitivity and relatively high cost. Therefore, producing blends of soy protein with some already-available biodegradable polyesters improves the water sensitivity and cost.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Some aliphatic polyesters

The [aliphatic](/wiki/Aliphatic "Aliphatic") bio[polyesters](/wiki/Polyester "Polyester") are mainly [polyhydroxyalkanoates](/wiki/Polyhydroxyalkanoate "Polyhydroxyalkanoate") (PHAs) like the [poly-3-hydroxybutyrate](/wiki/Poly-3-hydroxybutyrate "Poly-3-hydroxybutyrate") (PHB), polyhydroxyvalerate (PHV) and polyhydroxyhexanoate (PHH).

#### Polylactic acid (PLA)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Mulch_Film_made_of_PLA-Blend_Bio-Flex.jpg/250px-Mulch_Film_made_of_PLA-Blend_Bio-Flex.jpg)

[Polylactic acid](/wiki/Polylactic_acid "Polylactic acid") (PLA) is a [transparent plastic](/wiki/Transparent_plastic "Transparent plastic") produced from [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize")[[44]](#cite_note-smithsonianmag.com-44) or [dextrose](/wiki/Dextrose "Dextrose"). Superficially, it is similar to conventional petrochemical-based mass plastics like [PS](/wiki/Polystyrene "Polystyrene"). It is derived from plants, and it biodegrades under industrial composting conditions. Unfortunately, it exhibits inferior impact strength, thermal robustness, and barrier properties (blocking air transport across the membrane) compared to non-biodegradable plastics.[[45]](#cite_note-Ullmanns-45) PLA and PLA blends generally come in the form of granulates. PLA is used on a limited scale for the production of films, fibers, plastic containers, cups, and bottles. PLA is also the most common type of plastic [filament](/wiki/Fiber "Fiber") used for home [fused deposition modeling](/wiki/Fused_deposition_modeling "Fused deposition modeling") in 3D printers.

#### Poly-3-hydroxybutyrate

The [biopolymer](/wiki/Biopolymer "Biopolymer") [poly-3-hydroxybutyrate](/wiki/Polyhydroxybutyrate "Polyhydroxybutyrate") (PHB) is a [polyester](/wiki/Polyester "Polyester") produced by certain bacteria processing glucose, corn starch[[46]](#cite_note-bioplastic-innovation.com-46) or wastewater.[[47]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-47) Its characteristics are similar to those of the petroplastic [polypropylene](/wiki/Polypropylene "Polypropylene") (PP). PHB production is increasing. The [South American](/wiki/South_America "South America") [sugar](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar") industry, for example, has decided to expand PHB production to an industrial scale. PHB is distinguished primarily by its physical characteristics. It can be processed into a transparent film with a melting point higher than 130 degrees Celsius, and is biodegradable without residue.

### Polyhydroxyalkanoates

[Polyhydroxyalkanoates](/wiki/Polyhydroxyalkanoates "Polyhydroxyalkanoates") (PHA) are linear [polyesters](/wiki/Polyester "Polyester") produced in nature by [bacterial](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") [fermentation](/wiki/Fermentation_(biochemistry) "Fermentation (biochemistry)") of [sugar](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar") or [lipids](/wiki/Lipid "Lipid"). They are produced by the bacteria to store carbon and energy. In industrial production, the polyester is extracted and purified from the bacteria by optimizing the conditions for the fermentation of sugar. More than 150 different [monomers](/wiki/Monomer "Monomer") can be combined within this family to give materials with extremely different properties. PHA is more ductile and less elastic than other plastics, and it is also biodegradable. These plastics are being widely used in the medical industry.

### Polyamide 11

[PA 11](/wiki/Polyamide_11 "Polyamide 11") is a [biopolymer](/wiki/Biopolymer "Biopolymer") derived from natural oil. It is also known under the tradename Rilsan B, commercialized by [Arkema](/wiki/Arkema "Arkema"). PA 11 belongs to the technical polymers family and is not biodegradable. Its properties are similar to those of [PA 12](/wiki/Polyamide_12 "Polyamide 12"), although emissions of greenhouse gases and consumption of nonrenewable resources are reduced during its production. Its thermal resistance is also superior to that of PA 12. It is used in high-performance applications like automotive fuel lines, pneumatic airbrake tubing, electrical cable antitermite sheathing, flexible oil and gas pipes, control fluid umbilicals, sports shoes, electronic device components, and catheters.

A similar plastic is Polyamide 410 (PA 410), derived 70% from [castor oil](/wiki/Castor_oil "Castor oil"), under the trade name EcoPaXX, commercialized by DSM.[[48]](#cite_note-EcoPaXX-48)
PA 410 is a high-performance polyamide that combines the benefits of a high melting point (approx. 250 °C), low moisture absorption and excellent resistance to various chemical substances.

### Bio-derived polyethylene

The basic building block ([monomer](/wiki/Monomer "Monomer")) of [polyethylene](/wiki/Polyethylene "Polyethylene") is ethylene. Ethylene is chemically similar to, and can be derived from ethanol, which can be produced by fermentation of agricultural feedstocks such as sugar cane or corn. Bio-derived polyethylene is chemically and physically identical to traditional polyethylene – it does not biodegrade but can be recycled. The Brazilian chemicals group [Braskem](/wiki/Braskem "Braskem") claims that using its method of producing polyethylene from sugar cane ethanol captures (removes from the environment) 2.15 tonnes of CO2 per tonne of Green Polyethylene produced.

### Genetically modified feedstocks

With [GM](/wiki/Genetic_modification "Genetic modification") corn being a common feedstock, it is unsurprising that some bioplastics are made from this.

Under the bioplastics manufacturing technologies there is the "plant factory" model, which uses [genetically modified crops](/wiki/Genetically_modified_crops "Genetically modified crops") or [genetically modified bacteria](/wiki/Genetically_modified_bacterium "Genetically modified bacterium") to optimize efficiency.

### Polyhydroxyurethanes

The condensation of [polyamines](/wiki/Polyamine "Polyamine") and cyclic carbonates produces polyhydroxyurethanes.[[49]](#cite_note-From_Petrochemical_Polyurethanes_to_Biobased_Polyhydroxyurethanes-49) Unlike traditional cross-linked polyurethanes, cross-linked polyhydroxyurethanes are in principle amenable to recycling and reprocessing through dynamic transcarbamoylation reactions.[[50]](#cite_note-Mechanically_Activated,_Catalyst-Free_Polyhydroxyurethane_Vitrimers-50)

### Lipid derived polymers

A number bioplastic classes have been synthesized from [plant](/wiki/Vegetable_oil "Vegetable oil") and animal derived fats and oils.[[51]](#cite_note-51) [Polyurethanes](/wiki/Polyurethane "Polyurethane"),[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) [polyesters](/wiki/Polyester "Polyester"),[[54]](#cite_note-54) [epoxy resins](/wiki/Epoxy_resins "Epoxy resins")[[55]](#cite_note-55) and a number of other types of polymers have been developed with comparable properties to crude oil based materials. The recent development of [olefin metathesis](/wiki/Olefin_metathesis "Olefin metathesis") has opened a wide variety of feedstocks to economical conversion into biomonomers and polymers.[[56]](#cite_note-56) With the growing production of traditional vegetable oils as well as low cost [microalgae derived oils](/wiki/Algae_fuel "Algae fuel"),[[57]](#cite_note-57) there is huge potential for growth in this area.

In 2024, Lamanna et al. introduced oleogels based on [ethyl cellulose](/wiki/Ethyl_cellulose "Ethyl cellulose") and [vegetable oils](/wiki/Vegetable_oil "Vegetable oil") as a novel bioplastic, named OleoPlast.[[58]](#cite_note-58) This bioplastic exhibits thermoplastic behavior, offering both recyclability and biodegradability. The key advantages of OleoPlast include the ability to customize its mechanical and physical properties, as well as its compatibility with different processing techniques, such as [injection molding](/wiki/Injection_moulding "Injection moulding"), hot pressing, [extrusion](/wiki/Extrusion "Extrusion"), and [fused filament fabrication](/wiki/Fused_filament_fabrication "Fused filament fabrication").

## Environmental impact

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Bottle_made_from_Cellulose_Acetate_Biograde.JPG/250px-Bottle_made_from_Cellulose_Acetate_Biograde.JPG)

Materials such as starch, cellulose, wood, sugar and biomass are used as a substitute for fossil fuel resources to produce bioplastics; this makes the production of bioplastics a more sustainable activity compared to conventional plastic production.[[59]](#cite_note-Gironi_and_Piemonte-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) The environmental impact of bioplastics is often debated, as there are many different metrics for "greenness" (e.g., water use, energy use, deforestation, biodegradation, etc.).[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-BiodegradableEnvironmentalImpactAxion-62)[[63]](#cite_note-BiodegradableImpactMiles-63) Hence bioplastic environmental impacts are categorized into nonrenewable energy use, [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), [eutrophication](/wiki/Eutrophication "Eutrophication") and [acidification](/wiki/Freshwater_acidification "Freshwater acidification").[[64]](#cite_note-Weis_et_al-64) Bioplastic production significantly reduces [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") and decreases non-renewable energy consumption.[[59]](#cite_note-Gironi_and_Piemonte-59) Firms worldwide would also be able to increase the [environmental sustainability](/wiki/Environmental_sustainability "Environmental sustainability") of their products by using bioplastics [[65]](#cite_note-65)

Although bioplastics save more nonrenewable energy than conventional plastics and emit less greenhouse gasses compared to conventional plastics, bioplastics also have negative environmental impacts such as eutrophication and acidification.[[64]](#cite_note-Weis_et_al-64) Bioplastics induce higher eutrophication potentials than conventional plastics.[[64]](#cite_note-Weis_et_al-64) Biomass production during industrial farming practices causes nitrate and phosphate to filtrate into water bodies; this causes eutrophication, the process in which a body of water gains excessive richness of nutrients.[[64]](#cite_note-Weis_et_al-64) Eutrophication is a threat to water resources around the world since it causes harmful algal blooms that create oxygen dead zones, killing aquatic animals.[[66]](#cite_note-66) Bioplastics also increase acidification.[[64]](#cite_note-Weis_et_al-64) The high increase in eutrophication and acidification caused by bioplastics is also caused by using chemical fertilizer in the cultivation of renewable raw materials to produce bioplastics.[[59]](#cite_note-Gironi_and_Piemonte-59)

Other environmental impacts of bioplastics include exerting lower human and terrestrial [ecotoxicity](/wiki/Ecotoxicity "Ecotoxicity") and carcinogenic potentials compared to conventional plastics.[[64]](#cite_note-Weis_et_al-64) However, bioplastics exert higher aquatic ecotoxicity than conventional materials.[[64]](#cite_note-Weis_et_al-64) Bioplastics and other bio-based materials increase stratospheric [ozone](/wiki/Ozone "Ozone") depletion compared to conventional plastics; this is a result of [nitrous oxide](/wiki/Nitrous_oxide "Nitrous oxide") emissions during fertilizer application during industrial farming for biomass production.[[64]](#cite_note-Weis_et_al-64) Artificial fertilizers increase nitrous oxide emissions especially when the crop does not need all the nitrogen.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Minor environmental impacts of bioplastics include toxicity through using pesticides on the crops used to make bioplastics.[[59]](#cite_note-Gironi_and_Piemonte-59) Bioplastics also cause carbon dioxide emissions from harvesting vehicles.[[59]](#cite_note-Gironi_and_Piemonte-59) Other minor environmental impacts include high water consumption for biomass cultivation, soil erosion, [soil carbon](/wiki/Soil_carbon "Soil carbon") losses and [loss of biodiversity](/wiki/Loss_of_biodiversity "Loss of biodiversity"), and they are mainly are a result of land use associated with bioplastics.[[64]](#cite_note-Weis_et_al-64) Land use for bioplastics production leads to lost [carbon sequestration](/wiki/Carbon_sequestration "Carbon sequestration") and increases the carbon costs while diverting land from its existing uses [[68]](#cite_note-68)

Although bioplastics are extremely advantageous because they reduce non-renewable consumption and GHG emissions, they also negatively affect the environment through land and water consumption, using pesticide and fertilizer, eutrophication and acidification; hence one's preference for either bioplastics or conventional plastics depends on what one considers the most important environmental impact.[[59]](#cite_note-Gironi_and_Piemonte-59)

Another issue with bioplastics, is that some bioplastics are made from the edible parts of crops. This makes the bioplastics compete with food production because the crops that produce bioplastics can also be used to feed people.[[69]](#cite_note-Cho-69) These bioplastics are called "1st generation feedstock bioplastics".
2nd generation feedstock bioplastics use non-food crops (cellulosic feedstock) or waste materials from 1st generation feedstock (e.g. waste vegetable oil). Third generation feedstock bioplastics use [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae") as the feedstock.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### Biodegradation of Bioplastics

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Air_Pillow_made_of_PLA-Blend_Bio-Flex.jpg/250px-Air_Pillow_made_of_PLA-Blend_Bio-Flex.jpg)

Biodegradation of any plastic is a process that happens at solid/liquid interface whereby the enzymes in the liquid phase depolymerize the solid phase.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Certain types of bioplastics as well as conventional plastics containing additives are able to biodegrade.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Bioplastics are able to biodegrade in different environments hence they are more acceptable than conventional plastics.[[73]](#cite_note-Emadian_et_al-73) [Biodegradability](/wiki/Biodegradability "Biodegradability") of bioplastics occurs under various environmental conditions including soil, aquatic environments and compost.[[73]](#cite_note-Emadian_et_al-73) Both the structure and composition of biopolymer or bio-composite have an effect on the biodegradation process, hence changing the composition and structure might increase biodegradability.[[73]](#cite_note-Emadian_et_al-73) Soil and compost as environment conditions are more efficient in biodegradation due to their high microbial diversity.[[73]](#cite_note-Emadian_et_al-73) Composting not only biodegrades bioplastics efficiently but it also significantly reduces the emission of greenhouse gases.[[73]](#cite_note-Emadian_et_al-73) Biodegradability of bioplastics in compost environments can be upgraded by adding more soluble sugar and increasing temperature.[[73]](#cite_note-Emadian_et_al-73) Soil environments on the other hand have high diversity of microorganisms making it easier for biodegradation of bioplastics to occur.[[73]](#cite_note-Emadian_et_al-73) However, bioplastics in soil environments need higher temperatures and a longer time to biodegrade.[[73]](#cite_note-Emadian_et_al-73) Some bioplastics biodegrade more efficiently in water bodies and marine systems; however, this causes danger to marine ecosystems and freshwater.[[73]](#cite_note-Emadian_et_al-73) Hence it is accurate to conclude that biodegradation of bioplastics in water bodies which leads to the death of aquatic organisms and unhealthy water can be noted as one of the negative environmental impacts of bioplastics.

## Bioplastics for construction materials

The concept of bioplastics dates back to the early 20th century. However, significant advancements occurred in the 1980s and 1990s when researchers began developing biodegradable plastics from natural sources. The construction industry started to take notice of bioplastics' potential in the late 2000s, driven by the global push for greener building practices.

In recent years, bioplastics have seen considerable advancements in terms of durability, cost-effectiveness, and performance. Innovations in biopolymer blends and composites have made bioplastics more suitable for construction applications, ranging from insulation to structural components.

### Applications in Construction

### Benefits of Bioplastics in Construction Environmental Impact

### Economic Benefits

### Challenges and Limitations

### Future Prospects

The future of bioplastics in construction looks promising, with continued research and innovation likely to expand their applications and improve their performance.[[80]](#cite_note-80) As the construction industry increasingly embraces sustainability, bioplastics are poised to play a critical role in the development of eco-friendly building materials.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Bioplastics offer a sustainable and versatile alternative to traditional construction materials, with significant environmental and economic benefits. While challenges remain, particularly in terms of cost and performance, the ongoing advancements in bioplastic techno

enriched_points.expanded_content: Bioplastics are plastics made from renewable biomass sources, such as corn or sugarcane, and can be biodegradable or compostable, offering a sustainable alternative to traditional plastics.
Sustainable materials like bioplastics reduce reliance on finite fossil fuels and promote environmental stewardship, contributing to a circular economy and minimizing ecological footprints.
Traditional plastics are non-biodegradable, persist in ecosystems for centuries, pollute oceans, harm wildlife, and contribute to significant environmental degradation and climate change.

enriched_points.point_title: Potential of bio-plastics in reducing pollution
enriched_points.expanded_content: Bioplastics can significantly lower plastic pollution levels by decomposing naturally, thus mitigating the harmful impacts associated with conventional plastic waste on the environment.
